title: Introduction to Computer Systems
summary: A computer system is an integration of CPU, memory, I/O, and persistent storage connected by buses. This lesson introduces the Von Neumann architecture and the historical development of computing.
content_html:
What is a Computer System?

A computer system consists of interconnected hardware components that process, store, and transfer information:

ComponentFunctionSpeed/Size
CPU (Central Processing Unit)Executes instructionsFastest
RegistersUltra-fast temporary storage inside CPU~16-32 registers
RAM (Fast memory)Active program and data storageGBs
CacheBuffer between CPU and RAMMBs
Persistent storage (disk/SSD)Long-term storageTBs
I/O channelsKeyboard, screen, networkVaries
Von Neumann Architecture — Programs and data share the same memory. The CPU fetches instructions from memory, decodes them, then executes them sequentially. This is the architecture of virtually all modern computers.

Information Representation

All information in a computer is stored as groups of bits. How a 32-bit value is interpreted depends on context:

TypeExample (32-bit value)
Unsigned integer2686726
Signed integer-2686726
Floating point~0.003...
Memory pointer0x28FF2A
ASCII string"Hell..."

Historical Milestones

YearPerson/EventSignificance
1791-1871Charles BabbageAnalytical Engine — first programmable computer concept
1937Claude ShannonBoolean algebra applied to electronic circuits
1944Howard Aiken + Grace HopperHarvard Mark I — electromechanical computer
1945John Von NeumannStored program architecture
1946ENIACFirst general-purpose electronic digital computer
1958Jack KilbyIntegrated circuit (IC)

Moore's Law

Transistor density on an IC roughly doubles every 18 months, driving exponential growth in computing power. This trend held from the 1960s through to the early 2020s.

Modern context: Moore's Law is slowing as transistors approach atomic sizes. Future gains come from multi-core CPUs, GPUs, and specialised accelerators (AI chips).

exercises.title: CPU function
exercises.prompt: The primary function of the CPU is to:
exercises.options_json: ["Store programs permanently", "Execute instructions", "Manage input/output", "Cool the computer"]

exercises.title: Von Neumann
exercises.prompt: The key principle of Von Neumann architecture is:
exercises.options_json: ["Programs are stored in ROM", "Programs and data share the same memory", "Each program has its own CPU", "Only one instruction can run at a time"]
exercises.answer: Programs and data share the same memory

exercises.title: Moore's Law
exercises.prompt: Moore's Law states that transistor density approximately:
exercises.options_json: ["Doubles every 5 years", "Doubles every 18 months", "Triples every year", "Halves every 18 months"]

exercises.title: Historical figure
exercises.prompt: Who applied Boolean algebra to electronic circuits in 1937?

exercises.title: Data interpretation
exercises.prompt: A 32-bit value in memory can be interpreted as:
exercises.options_json: ["Only an integer", "Only a float", "Only a string", "An integer, float, pointer, or string depending on context"]
exercises.answer: An integer, float, pointer, or string depending on context

exercises.title: Stored program
exercises.prompt: The stored program concept was formalised by:

exercises.title: ENIAC
exercises.prompt: ENIAC (1946) was significant because it was:
exercises.options_json: ["The first transistor-based computer", "The first general-purpose electronic digital computer", "The first personal computer", "The first stored-program computer"]
exercises.answer: The first general-purpose electronic digital computer

exercises.title: Fastest storage
exercises.prompt: Which component provides the fastest storage in a computer system?

exercises.title: Cache purpose
exercises.options_json: ["Long-term storage", "A buffer between CPU and RAM to reduce access latency", "The main memory for programs", "I/O controller"]
exercises.answer: A buffer between CPU and RAM to reduce access latency
